(1) references/*.md citations. Enumerated every `references/` mention in the 9 SKILL.md files (skills/*/SKILL.md) and the 18 commands/*.md files via `grep -no "references/"`.
- skills/atlas-engine/SKILL.md and skills/atlas-expedition/SKILL.md cite files inside their own `references/` dirs (both exist: skills/atlas-engine/references/*.md has 19 files, skills/atlas-expedition/references/*.md + references/roles/*.md has 11 files). - All 15 launcher commands (atlas-component.md, atlas-db-audit.md, atlas-debug.md, atlas-feature.md, atlas-grafana.md, atlas-frontend.md, atlas-gitignore.md, atlas-handoff.md, atlas-m365.md, atlas-launch.md, atlas-harden.md, atlas-refactor.md, atlas-readme.md, atlas-validate.md, atlas-vendor-assessment.md) cite `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/atlas-engine/references/operating-contract.md` at lines 9 and 12 each (30 citations) - resolves against plugin root.

- README.md:86,158 mention operating-contract.md, capability-catalog.md, and `skills/atlas-engine/references/hooks-automation.md` - all resolve.

In every case the clearly-intended target (skills/atlas-engine/references/workflow-template.md, skills/atlas-engine/references/docs-ssot.md) exists on disk, so I did not count these as dangling (the target file exists), but flagging them as inconsistent citation forms worth a documentation fix - see dangling list below is empty because targets resolve; these three are noted as caveats, not failures.

(3) hooks/hooks.json script paths. All 9 distinct script commands (used across 12 hook-event entries: SessionStart x2, UserPromptSubmit x1, PreToolUse x1, PostToolUse x2, Stop x3, SubagentStop x2, SessionEnd x1, PreCompact x1) exist on disk, verified with direct `-f` checks: hooks/session_boot.py, scripts/atlas_doctor.py, hooks/prompt_optimizer.py, hooks/bash_advisor.py, hooks/format_after_edit.py, hooks/dispatch_tripwire.py, hooks/completion_gate.py, hooks/ingest_session.py, hooks/nudge.py. The only issue found is a cosmetic/documentation one: three cross-file citations (atlas-cartographer/SKILL.md:24, atlas-survey/SKILL.md:97, commands/atlas.md:58) use an inconsistent relative-path form that would not literally resolve if read as a strict relative path from the citing file, even though the clearly-intended target file exists elsewhere in the tree.
unverified: skills/atlas-cartographer/SKILL.md:24 cites "atlas-engine/references/workflow-template.md" - literal relative-path form does not resolve from the citing file's own directory (would need ../atlas-engine/...); the named target exists at skills/atlas-engine/references/workflow-template.md so I did not mark it dangling, but I could not confirm the author intended a bare cross-skill path vs. a typo'd relative path.

commands/atlas.md:58 cites bare "references/docs-ssot.md" with no directory prefix; commands/ has no references/ dir of its own, so this only resolves if read as shorthand for skills/atlas-engine/references/docs-ssot.md (which exists) - intent not fully verifiable from the text alone.

Could not verify at a semantic level whether every non-atlas: agent-type name in capability-routing.md (e.g. debugger, security-engineer, code-reviewer) actually corresponds to a live Claude Code built-in or externally-installed agent/plugin on this machine - I verified only that they are absent from this plugin's agents/ dir, and classified them as external per the task's own example list and the file's stated intent ("always prefer a live-discovered better fit").

a2.summary: Audited the active atlas plugin version (2.6.0, confirmed as the non-orphaned install at /Users/jerry/.claude/plugins/cache/tech-tools/atlas/2.6.0 - 2.4.0 and 2.5.0 both carry .orphaned_at timestamps, 2.6.0 has none). hooks/hooks.json declares 8 event types (SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SubagentStop, SessionEnd, PreCompact) across 9 matcher blocks totaling 13 command entries, referencing 9 distinct scripts, all of which exist and pass py_compile with rc=0. Ran live fail-open tests against dispatch_tripwire.py, completion_gate.py, and prompt_optimizer.py with malformed JSON and JSON missing expected fields: all three returned exit 0 with empty stdout in every case (6/6 invocations), proving fail-open. Then read and empirically confirmed the orchestration-marker gating in both gate scripts using synthetic DB state (isolated via ATLAS_DB env var to a scratchpad SQLite file, never touching ~/.atlas) and synthetic scratchpad project dirs: dispatch_tripwire.py keys on atlas_db.is_orchestrating (line 93) and completion_gate.py keys on two markers in series, docs/ presence (lines 244-245) then atlas_db.is_orchestrating (lines 246-247).

findings.claim: hooks/hooks.json (2.6.0) declares 8 event types across 9 matcher blocks totaling 13 command entries, referencing 9 distinct scripts, all present and syntactically valid.
findings.evidence: Read of atlas/2.6.0/hooks/hooks.json lines 1-118: SessionStart 2 hooks (session_boot.py, scripts/atlas_doctor.py), UserPromptSubmit 1 (prompt_optimizer.py), PreToolUse 1 (bash_advisor.py), PostToolUse 2 matcher blocks / 2 hooks (format_after_edit.py, dispatch_tripwire.py), Stop 3 (completion_gate.py, ingest_session.py, nudge.py), SubagentStop 2 (ingest_session.py, nudge.py), SessionEnd 1 (ingest_session.py), PreCompact 1 (ingest_session.py). Bash loop ran python3 -m py_compile against all 9 distinct script paths; every file exists=yes and py_compile rc=0 for each.

findings.claim: dispatch_tripwire.py, completion_gate.py, and prompt_optimizer.py all fail open on malformed JSON stdin: exit 0, empty stdout.
findings.evidence: Command printf malformed-json-string piped into each of the three scripts. Result for all three: exit_code=0 and stdout equal to empty string. Code basis: dispatch_tripwire.py wraps main() in a bare except Exception then calls sys.exit(0) unconditionally at hooks/dispatch_tripwire.py lines 116-120; completion_gate.py catches JSONDecodeError/ValueError and returns 0 at hooks/completion_gate.py lines 230-231, plus an outer except Exception returning 0 at lines 265-267; prompt_optimizer.py catches JSONDecodeError/ValueError and returns 0 at hooks/prompt_optimizer.py lines 327-328.

findings.claim: All three scripts also fail open on syntactically valid JSON missing all expected fields, including an empty object and a partial payload with tool_name present but tool_input absent.
findings.evidence: printf empty-json-object piped into each of the three scripts produced exit_code=0 and empty stdout in every case. A follow-up payload containing only session_id and tool_name (no tool_input) piped into dispatch_tripwire.py and completion_gate.py also produced exit_code=0 and empty stdout for both.

findings.claim: dispatch_tripwire.py orchestration marker is atlas_db.is_orchestrating keyed by session_id; without it the STOP nag never fires even once the inline-op threshold is exceeded on a non-docs edit path.
findings.evidence: Set ATLAS_DB to a scratchpad-only SQLite file, created a run for a synthetic session via atlas_db.start_run with orchestrating left at its default of 0, confirmed atlas_db.is_orchestrating returned False. Sent 5 sequential Edit tool_input events targeting a scratchpad path outside any docs tree: all 5 calls returned exit=0 and empty output despite the inline-op count exceeding the default threshold of 4 and edit_to_target evaluating True. Positive control: called atlas_db.mark_orchestrating to flip the flag to True, confirmed via is_orchestrating, then repeated the identical Edit event: output changed to a hookSpecificOutput additionalContext STOP message naming the edited path and telling the orchestrator to route it to atlas:implementer. This isolates the marker branch to hooks/dispatch_tripwire.py lines 92 through 94, the block reading roughly if count is at or above threshold or edit_to_target is true, then if not atlas_db.is_orchestrating, return. Note the STOP message is advisory context injected into a PostToolUse event, not a blocking exit code; the hook exits 0 in both the gated and un-gated cases.

findings.claim: completion_gate.py keys on two markers evaluated in series, docs directory presence then atlas_db.is_orchestrating, and no-ops with exit 0 and empty stdout when either is absent.
findings.evidence: Test A, docs absent: synthetic cwd with no docs subdirectory produced exit=0 and empty output. Test B, docs present but session not orchestrating: synthetic cwd with an empty docs subdirectory, confirmed atlas_db.is_orchestrating returned False for that session (no run ever created for it), produced exit=0 and empty output. Positive control: called atlas_db.mark_orchestrating to flip both markers true for that same session and cwd, re-ran against the same still-empty docs directory (so the six done-conditions remain unmet), and stdout changed to a JSON object with decision block and a reason string listing the unmet conditions a through e. This isolates the two-marker check to hooks/completion_gate.py line 244 through 245, where docs equals _find_docs of cwd and the function returns 0 if docs is None, and lines 246 through 247, where the function returns 0 if the session is not flagged orchestrating.

findings.claim: dispatch_tripwire.py defines an ORCH_MARKERS constant that appears to be dead code, never referenced anywhere else in the file.
findings.evidence: A grep for ORCH_MARKERS across hooks/dispatch_tripwire.py returns only its own definition at line 15, tuple containing the string docs slash, with no other match anywhere in the file. The actual docs-path check used for gating is a separate inline literal inside the _is_orchestration_path helper at lines 36 through 40, not this constant.

unverified: Whether the completion_gate.py block JSON with a decision of block actually causes Claude Code to re-prompt or block the Stop event in the live harness - only the script's own stdout and exit code were verified, not the harness's interpretation of that JSON contract, which would require an actual Stop-hook-triggering session and falls outside this read-only synthetic-fixture check.

Whether any concurrent process wrote to the real ~/.atlas/atlas.db during this audit - that file showed an mtime in the same minute as the test runs, but every test command explicitly set ATLAS_DB to a scratchpad path and the resulting database files were confirmed present only in scratchpad via a directory listing, so the shared mtime is attributed to unrelated concurrent activity rather than these commands, though the process responsible was not independently traced.

Whether hooks/bash_advisor.py, hooks/format_after_edit.py, hooks/session_boot.py, hooks/ingest_session.py, hooks/nudge.py, and scripts/atlas_doctor.py are themselves fail-open under malformed or missing-field stdin - the task scoped the fail-open proof to only dispatch_tripwire.py, completion_gate.py, and the UserPromptSubmit optimizer prompt_optimizer.py, so the other six referenced scripts were verified only for existence and py_compile success, not runtime fail-open behavior.
a2.next_step: If broader hook-safety coverage is wanted, repeat the same malformed-JSON and missing-field stdin fail-open probes against bash_advisor.py (PreToolUse, blocking-capable and highest risk if it fails closed), format_after_edit.py, session_boot.py, ingest_session.py, nudge.py, and scripts/atlas_doctor.py --hook, since those were only compile-checked here, not runtime-tested.

Full pass (all three controls explicitly present, same or adjacent sentences): api-usage-map.md (line 13: "record it as UNVERIFIED instead of guessing" + file:line at lines 13/15) and naming-glossary-audit.md (line 17: "mark it UNVERIFIED" + file:line evidence requirement at line 13).
Net: 1 full miss, 14 partial (at least one control), 2 full pass.

(4) fourpart_status: CONFIRMED for atlas-engine only. skills/atlas-engine/references/subagent-kit.md:27-32 states explicitly: "The spec above is the full form. Its load-bearing core is the 4-part brief, never dispatch without all four: 1. Task (GOAL) 2. Product (DELIVERABLE) 3. Where to save (path outputs land in) 4. Prior context (CONTEXT: only what the agent cannot derive itself)." This reference is loaded by skills/atlas-engine/SKILL.md:181 ("writing any subagent dispatch (spec template, output contract, per-role briefs)"). However, this four-part mandate lives in a references/ file, not inline in SKILL.md itself, and I found NO equivalent four-part dispatch-brief template or requirement, inline or by reference, in any of the other 8 skills' SKILL.md files (atlas-architect, atlas-cartographer, atlas-expedition, atlas-harbor, atlas-orbit, atlas-sextant, atlas-stacks, atlas-survey) - grep for GOAL:/DELIVERABLE:/CONTEXT:/ROLE:/"4-part"/"subagent-kit" across those 8 files returned zero hits except one unrelated "CONTEXT:" line in atlas-sextant's memory-capture template (SKILL.md:291, a claude-mem TYPE/CONTEXT/LESSON/SOURCE format, not a subagent dispatch brief). So: only atlas-engine's skill machinery defines and requires the four-part brief; the other skills that also fan out subagents (survey, cartographer, expedition, orbit) do not carry their own explicit four-part requirement and presumably rely on borrowing atlas-engine's convention, but that reliance is not written down anywhere I could find - flagging as unverified whether they inherit it at runtime or just dispatch ad hoc.

(5) standing_consent_hits_outside_engine: ZERO hits outside skills/atlas-engine/. Grepping the entire 2.6.0 plugin tree for "standing consent"/"standing-consent"/"orchestration mode" returns exactly two files, both inside skills/atlas-engine/: SKILL.md:18-20 ("## Standing-consent orchestration mode" / "you have standing consent to fan out ... without re-asking permission each time") and references/multi-stage-planning.md:70-75 (same contract, plus "Saying 'mode on' - or re-invoking the skill - restores standing consent"). No other agent spec or skill file anywhere in the plugin contains this language.
a4.fourpart_status: CONFIRMED for atlas-engine only, via skills/atlas-engine/references/subagent-kit.md:27-32 (loaded from SKILL.md:181): "load-bearing core is the 4-part brief, never dispatch without all four: Task/Product/Where to save/Prior context." No other skill's SKILL.md (architect, cartographer, expedition, harbor, orbit, sextant, stacks, survey) contains an equivalent explicit four-part dispatch-brief requirement (grep for GOAL:/DELIVERABLE:/CONTEXT:/4-part/subagent-kit returned zero relevant hits in those 8 files). Whether those other skills inherit the atlas-engine four-part convention at runtime is unverified - not written down anywhere found.
unverified: Whether 'undefined/agents' and 'undefined/skills' literally refer to /Users/jerry/.claude/plugins/cache/tech-tools/atlas/2.6.0 (the substitution made here) or to some other repo/version not present in this environment - no literal 'undefined' directory was found by find(1).

Whether skills atlas-survey, atlas-cartographer, atlas-expedition, and atlas-orbit (which all fan out subagents per their descriptions) actually apply atlas-engine's four-part dispatch brief at runtime, since none of their SKILL.md files state this explicitly or reference subagent-kit.md.
Whether the 2.4.0 and 2.5.0 versions of the atlas plugin (also present in the plugin cache) differ on any of these findings - only 2.6.0 was audited.

Whether 'partial' hallucination-control files (14 of 17) should be treated as passing or failing conformance - the task defines them as partial, not pass/fail, so no verdict is asserted here beyond the per-file control breakdown given.
